FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 4262 children ages 9-11 from 21 recruitment sites, Release 2.0 baseline data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cohort study with genetic sensitivity analyses (Gsens) incorporating polygenic risk scores (PRSs) and heritability estimates to assess genetic confounding in screen time associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open, November 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (IV, measured child-reported daily hours) paired with psychiatric problems (DV): attention problems (β=0.10 SD) and internalizing problems (β=0.03 SD), both individual-trait-level outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: screen time associations with attention (β=0.10 SD, 95% CI 0.07-0.13 SD) and internalizing (β=0.03 SD, 95% CI 0.003-0.06 SD); PRS associations; genetic confounding proportions (42.7% for internalizing)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis confirmed with reported effect estimates, confidence intervals, and p-values for the relationship between screen time and psychiatric outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional study; FT-EC7 does not apply
notes: Study uses baseline ABCD data only; no longitudinal follow-up analysis. Genetic confounding accounts for much of the observed association.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, longitudinal cohort with baseline and two-year follow-up structural imaging data, 5,166 participants ages 9-10

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal study with mediation analyses using Joint and Individual Variation Explained (JIVE) and generalized linear mixed-effect models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA, IV) at baseline paired with internalizing psychopathology (DV) at two-year follow-up (β=0.020, SE=0.008, p=0.014), mediated by brain structural co-development pattern (neural mediator, not demographic or contextual)

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported: SMA to internalizing (β=0.020, p=0.014); SMA to structural co-development (β=0.015, p=0.029); indirect effect = 0.020, p=0.043, proportion mediated 2.24%

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential mediation analysis with beta coefficients, standard errors, and p-values reported for direct and indirect pathways

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with SMA as IV (predictor) in main analysis; FT-EC7 does not apply (SM is not DV-only)
notes: Mediation effect size small but significant. Brain co-development pattern includes gray-matter volume changes in brainstem, superior/rostral middle frontal, inferior parietal, and inferior temporal regions.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, baseline data (2017-2018), 11,780 youth ages 9-10 across 21 multi-site recruitment centers

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional study with child-reported screen time and semi-structured clinical interview (K-SADS-5) measuring DSM-5 diagnoses of internalizing disorders

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Affective Disorders, 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time composite (TV, online videos, gaming, texting, social media, video chatting; IV) paired with internalizing disorder diagnoses (depressive, anxiety, suicidal ideation/attempt, self-harm; DV). All outcomes are individual-trait-level psychiatric diagnoses measured by K-SADS-5

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric results for screen time associations with each internalizing diagnosis: depressive (girls ARR=2.00, 95% CI 1.48-2.70; boys ARR=1.66, 95% CI 1.22-2.25); anxiety, suicidal ideation, self-harm all with reported RR/ARR and 95% CIs

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses with adjusted relative risk ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values reported for screen time measures against each DSM-5 diagnosis criterion

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional baseline design; FT-EC7 does not apply (no longitudinal design)
notes: Study examined exposure-response curves showing dose-dependent associations. Separate analyses by gender. Digital media use (social media, texting, gaming, online videos) showed stronger associations with anxiety than total screen time.
